methods.description: The AFT method is based on the quantification of damage to the crystal lattice (tracks) that results from the spontaneous fission of 238 U. These tracks are partially annealed at temperatures between ~60°C and 120°C; this interval is known as the apatite partial annealing zone (APAZ). Fission tracks shorten within the APAZ; therefore, the track length distribution (TLD) and the mean track length (MTL) values can be used as proxies to reconstruct the thermal history within the APAZ. The fission-track annealing resistance depends strongly on the kinetics of the apatite crystal. A useful kinetic indicator is the resistance of the crystal to the acid used to reveal the tracks (etching). Therefore, the diameter of the fission-track etch pit (Dpar) can be used as a proxy to quantify resistance to annealing.

steps.description: Quantify damage to the crystal lattice (tracks) from the spontaneous fission of 238 U.
steps.searchable_summary: quantify crystal lattice damage from 238 U fission

steps.description: Measure the diameter of the fission-track etch pit (Dpar) to quantify resistance to annealing.

methods.description: AHe thermochronology quantifies the production and accumulation of helium formed by the alpha decay of U, Th, and Sm. The temperature interval in which apatite retains helium is known as the apatite partial retention zone (APRZ). The AHe closure temperature is controlled by the helium retentivity of the crystal, which can be affected by accumulated radiation damage and the effective uranium content (eU). The size and geometry of the crystals also affect helium retentivity.

steps.description: Quantify helium production and accumulation from alpha decay of U, Th, and Sm.
Analyze the effect of accumulated radiation damage and effective uranium content (eU) on helium retentivity.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze radiation damage and eU effect on helium retentivity

methods.method_name: Summary of AFT and AHe Data
methods.description: Inverse modeling with the QTQt program to extract the most probable thermal history from robust data sets. AHe single grain ages that cannot be explained by available diffusion models are identified and excluded from the thermal history models. The radiation damage model from Flowers et al. (2009) for AHe data and the annealing model of Ketcham et al. (2007) for AFT data are used.

steps.description: Perform inverse modeling with the QTQt program using a Bayesian transdimensional statistical approach.
steps.searchable_summary: perform inverse modeling with QTQt for thermal history

steps.description: Identify and exclude AHe single grain ages that cannot be explained by available diffusion models.
steps.searchable_summary: exclude unexplained AHe ages from models

steps.description: Use the radiation damage model from Flowers et al. (2009) for AHe data and the annealing model of Ketcham et al. (2007) for AFT data.
steps.searchable_summary: apply radiation damage and annealing models
